name: Alchemy Overview
description: Alchemy is a philosophical and protoscientific tradition practiced throughout history in various parts of the world. It aims to purify, mature, and perfect certain objects. Common goals included the transmutation of base metals into noble metals (gold being the most sought after), the creation of an elixir of immortality, and the creation of panaceas able to cure any disease. While often associated with mysticism and the occult, early alchemists saw their work as a practical application of philosophical and religious principles. Western alchemy, tracing its roots to Hellenistic Egypt, draws heavily from Hermeticism, Gnosticism, and Neoplatonism. Key figures include figures attributed to Hermes Trismegistus, and later figures such as Jabir ibn Hayyan (Geber). Eastern alchemy, particularly Chinese alchemy (Waidan and Neidan), focused on achieving physical immortality through the ingestion of specially prepared substances or through internal practices such as meditation and Qigong. Indian alchemy (Rasayana) shared similar goals. Mainstream science views alchemy as a pseudoscience, lacking empirical validation for its core claims. It acknowledges that alchemy contributed to the development of chemistry and pharmacology by developing laboratory techniques and discovering chemical substances. Critics point to the lack of reproducible results and the reliance on symbolic language as evidence of alchemy's unscientific nature. Proponents argue that alchemy should be understood as a spiritual discipline rather than a purely scientific endeavor, and that its symbolic language reflects deeper truths about the nature of reality. Some modern researchers explore the potential for unconventional chemical reactions or energy transformations within the framework of alchemical principles, although these remain outside mainstream scientific acceptance.
subtitle: Transforming matter and the self.

claims.claim: Base metals can be transmuted into gold.
claims.evidence: Alchemical texts detail procedures for transmutation, often involving the Philosopher's Stone.
claims.criticism: No reproducible scientific evidence exists to support the transmutation of elements through alchemical means. Nuclear transmutation is possible, but requires extreme conditions and advanced technology.

claims.claim: An elixir of immortality can be created.
claims.evidence: Alchemical texts describe elixirs that can prolong life indefinitely or even grant immortality.
claims.criticism: No scientifically validated elixir of immortality has ever been created. Aging is a complex biological process that is not fully understood.

claims.claim: Alchemy can lead to spiritual enlightenment.
claims.evidence: Alchemical processes are often interpreted as metaphors for inner transformation and spiritual growth.
claims.criticism: Spiritual experiences are subjective and cannot be objectively verified through scientific methods. The connection between alchemical symbolism and spiritual enlightenment is a matter of personal interpretation.

keyFigures.name: Hermes Trismegistus
keyFigures.role: Legendary founder of Hermeticism, a key influence on Western alchemy

keyFigures.name: Jabir ibn Hayyan (Geber)
keyFigures.role: Prominent Islamic alchemist, contributed to chemical knowledge and apparatus

keyFigures.name: Paracelsus
keyFigures.role: Swiss-German physician and alchemist, emphasized the use of alchemy in medicine

keyFigures.name: Antoine Lavoisier
keyFigures.role: French chemist, whose work helped dismantle alchemy as a science

mythologyConnections: Various creation myths involving the transformation of matter

timeline.event: Emergence of Hermetic texts in Hellenistic Egypt
Development of alchemy in the Islamic world
Translation of Arabic alchemical texts into Latin, spreading alchemy in Europe
Flourishing of alchemy in Europe, associated with figures like Paracelsus and John Dee
Decline of alchemy as chemistry emerges as a distinct science

sources.author: Marie-Louise von Franz
sources.work: Alchemy: An Introduction to the Symbolism and the Psychology
sources.title: Marie-Louise von Franz
